AMMONIHAH: When Alma had made the three days' journey spoken of in Alma 8:6, he reached Ammonihah, the country around which city was called by the same name. From the text of the passage referred to, some conclude that Alma traveled northward from Melek, but to us it conveys the idea that the prophet journeyed three days westward along or near the northern boundary of that land. We are confirmed in this opinion by the statement that is made in another place regarding Ammonihah's proximity to…

should go into the wilderness in search of their brethren," knowing that Alma "had the spirit of prophecy" (Alma 16:5). Captain Moroni was sure to consult with Alma the prophet and high priest before going into battle in the land of Manti, and the word of the Lord delivered by Alma told Moroni when and where to meet and defeat the enemy (see Alma 43:22-24). Later, chief captains were appointed who "had the spirit of revelation and also prophecy" (3 Nephi 3:19). (John W. Welch in Stephen D.…
Moroni is “appointed to be the chief captain” implying there are other captains.

He was only twenty and five years old when he was appointed chief captain over the armies of the Nephites. And it came to pass that he met the Lamanites in the borders of Jershon, and his people were armed with swords and with cimeters, and all manner of weapons of war.

Sidon. (1) The river Sidon ran by the land of Zarahemla (Alma 2:15), an expression which seems to indicate that its course was near a border rather than in the center of the country. The valley of Gideon was on the east bank and the city of Zarahemla was on the west. (Alma 6:7; 2:26, 27.) The land of Manti was south of Gideon. (Alma 16: 6; 22:27.) Through the borders of Manti ran the narrow strip of wilderness or barren hills that separated Zarahemla from the land of Nephi along the entire…

The land of Ammonihah] The city of Ammonihah and "every living soul of the Ammonihahites" were destroyed because they martyred the Saints and persecuted the prophets (see Alma 16:9). (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 3:, p.173)

The land of Ammonihah] The city of Ammonihah and "every living soul of the Ammonihahites" were destroyed because they martyred the Saints and persecuted the prophets (see Alma 16:9). (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 3:, p.173)

Only once in the Book of Mormon do we read of a case of annihilation, when we are specifically told that "every living soul of the Ammonihahites was destroyed" (Alma 16:9), where not only the social structure but each individual is undone. In other instances the Lord promises that he will not utterly destroy the descendants of Lehi's youngest son, Joseph (2 Nephi 3:3), or of Lemuel (2 Nephi 4:9), and even Nephi is

him, so they must repent; and if they fail to do so they will be scattered, he prophesies, and become "meat for dogs and wild beasts." (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 3:, p.369)

vultures, dogs, wild beasts All of these animals were unclean under the Law of Moses. The vulture (Heb. 'ayah) is from the root word 'iy meaning alas or woe. The dog (Heb. keleb) was figurative of contempt or abasement. Wild beasts (Heb. chay) from the root word chayah meaning sickness. This is a very strong statement by Abinadi.

The account of Alma, who was the son of Alma, the first and chief judge over the people of Nephi, and also the high priest over the Church. An account of the reign of the judges, and the wars and contentions among the people. And also an account of a war between the Nephites and the Lamanites, according to the record of Alma, the first and chief judge.

A branch ... grafted into the true vine] "I AM the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman," Christ declared. "I am the vine, ye are the branches: He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing. If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered; and men gather them, and cast them into the fire, and they are burned." (John 15:1, 5.) One is grafted into the true branch when he or she comes to the knowledge of the…

The word 'fellowship' (koinonia) comes three times this section (1:3, 1:6, 1:7). In the NT it is a partnership in the work - we have been grafted into the true vine and we have a promise of inheritance (Theological Dictionary of the NT, 449)

The word 'fellowship' (koinonia) comes three times this section (1:3, 1:6, 1:7). In the NT it is a partnership in the work - we have been grafted into the true vine and we have a promise of inheritance (Theological Dictionary of the NT, 449)

A branch ... grafted into the true vine] "I AM the true vine, and my Father is the husbandman," Christ declared. "I am the vine, ye are the branches: He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing. If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch, and is withered; and men gather them, and cast them into the fire, and they are burned." (John 15:1, 5.) One is grafted into the true branch when he or she comes to the knowledge of the…

The word 'fellowship' (koinonia) comes three times this section (1:3, 1:6, 1:7). In the NT it is a partnership in the work - we have been grafted into the true vine and we have a promise of inheritance (Theological Dictionary of the NT, 449)

Eleven times in the lists of laws and sins throughout the Book of Mormon we find the English terms "lie" or "lying" (2 Ne. 26:32; Alma 1:17; 16:18; Hel. 4:12; 3 Ne. 16:10; 21:19; 30:2; Ether 8:16; Morm. 8:31). In five of these cases "lie" is accompanied by "deceive" (Alma 16:18; 3 Ne. 16:10; 21:19; 30:2; Morm. 8:31). The term "lying" admittedly represents a larger category of sins than "bearing false witness against one's neighbor," but it is likely that this term, as it is found in the lists…

Holding forth] That is, believing in, looking forward to, anticipating anxiously. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 3:, p.123)
